title: [docs] Distinguish between lazy pulling and partial pulling
body:
Currently "partial pulling" and "lazy pulling" are used interchangeably, e.g. here:

https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/e79686ee12eb64bd296625b6a55381554d4080cc/docs/tutorials/performance.md#lazy-pulling-of-container-images

> Lazy pulling of container images
>
> Podman supports lazy pulling for the following container image formats:
>
> - zstd:chunked
> - eStargz
> 
> zstd:chunked has better performance than eStargz.
>
> See the article [Pull container images faster with partial pulls](https://www.redhat.com/sysadmin/faster-container-image-pulls) by Giuseppe Scrivano and Dan Walsh.

However, the wider container ecosystem seems to use these 2 terms for 2 different things. Partial pulling means the tool doesn't need to download chunks it already downloaded before (chunk cache is reused between pulls). Lazy pulling means the container can be started before it finishes downloading, and its chunks will be downloaded on-demand.

As far as I know, podman supports "partial pulling", but not "lazy pulling". I.e. the image download process does not need to download all the data from scratch, but it still needs to finish before a container can be started. Am I right?

Meanwhile containerd (nerdctl) supports "lazy pulling", but doesn't mention anything about "partial pulling" (as in: cache reuse between downloads). So it can start a container having downloaded only the files the image needs (nice), but if two different images share the same files, it will (probably) still end up downloading these files twice.

If my reasoning is correct, can we document this better?

---

By the way: calling cache reuse "partial pulling" is a bit unfortunate here, since by the sound of it, "lazy pulling" should be partial (it only downloads parts of the images that are used), but with current meaning of these 2 phrases, it's not. Maybe we need another name for "partial pulling", like "pulling with chunk-level cache"?

comments.body:
podman does not support "lazy pulling" by itself, but it needs https://github.com/containerd/stargz-snapshotter configured as an additional layer store.

I think we need to replace each occurrence of "lazy pulling" with "partial pulling"

> podman does not support "lazy pulling" by itself, but it needs https://github.com/containerd/stargz-snapshotter configured as an additional layer store.

Oh, I missed that. Great to see that!

By the way stargz-snapshotter now has a zstdchunked mode. Does it mean that podman+stargz-snapshotter can lazy-pull a zstd:chunked image, or was this not integrated yet?
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

I've been needing to get this done months ago, I'm finally putting attention to it.

I was able to setup podman with stargz store plugin.


I was initially after partial pulling, becuase I run a 3G connection so I have slow and unstable internet.

does this mean I need zstdchunk, I heard about it I haven't been able to look into it.

so for example I have stargz store setup now, how would I download ollama container?

will stargz help with my unstable slow connection?

Stock podman looses pretty much loses all progress when the connection drops, it will even lose blobs that already completed!

btw podman and CRI-O (dont' really know what that is yet), uses Stargz "STORE PLUGIN" which is basically this part

/etc/containers/storage.conf

```

[storage.options]
additionallayerstores = ["/var/lib/stargz-store/store:ref"]
```

So sounds confusing, it's basically a stargz variable set in the storage conf that podman uses.

store plugin makes it sound like it's some kind of plugin that connects to some stargz repo, like it's a stargz app store. it's a configuration for storage under a section, a plugin, in the config file.

To simplify even further, this step just enables stargz, podman points at some stargz thing, that's all.

apparently you can use stores to for example have podman on different client pull from a specific directoy and share images

https://www.redhat.com/en/blog/image-stores-podman

found this 

November 12, 2021
https://www.redhat.com/en/blog/faster-container-image-pulls

ok so looks like "partial pulls" have the benefit of not needing to pull the entire image when updating, instead pulling only the blobs that are updated

"Worse, every time the Fedora image—or any image—updates, you have to pull the entire image again, not just the differences."


"Another thing to think about is kernel memory. The Linux kernel is smart enough to know that if two different processes load the same container (such as a shared library) into memory, they should load it into memory just once. For example, if you have 10 different programs running simultaneously that use libc, the /usr/lib/libc.so.6 code loads into kernel memory only once. When you run containers with images, if the same /usr/lib/libc.so.6 is in multiple different images, then the kernel gets confused and loads several versions of the same content into memory, wasting resources. Because of the way we currently store container images, this is very common.

"

edit: ok looks like you linked this in the OP, well here's more info on that

Just sharing some information I've come across, the eStargz `zstd:chunked` support isn't well documented yet compared to `gzip`, and I've not yet got around to trying to verify myself.
- [This `nerdctl` document](https://github.com/containerd/nerdctl/blob/main/docs/stargz.md#tips-3-using-zstd-instead-of-gzip-aka-zstdchunked) mentions unlike `gzip` the support for some eStargz features (_external TOC and min chunk size options_) are not supported for `zstd:chunked` image conversion. So if used with `gzip` conversion be aware any advantages with that would not apply with the zstd equivalent when comparing the two with eStargz.
- `zstd:chunked` vs `zstd` compressed images makes the most difference when you've already got the content locally to avoid pulling, and most container engines / clients (common popular ones at least) should have support for `zstd:chunked` optimization, but the convenience with base image content being shared would probably apply most when you can ensure it's a consistent base image digest, otherwise AFAIK should mtime change on files despite otherwise equivalent content you may not get a cache hit (_as is the case with layer digests_).
- eStargz or similar alternatives provide the convenience of being able to start earlier, but may not be appropriate to rely on on-demand pulling of content when the software in the container needs to perform many imports as you'd incur notable latency overhead of each file being requested individually which then once available need to load additional files, sending another request and so forth. Profiling access or preparing a prefetch list may be viable, but it may also be inconvenient if that is quite dynamic based on user workload (nodejs / python runtimes). One user with an AI focused image found the image quick to start but runtime from the entrypoint onwards took over 4 minutes, as opposed to approx 2 minutes when pulling all layers upfront.

When there's no local cache to leverage, the client may be able to better parallelize pulling content with the HTTP range requests for `zstd:chunked` than entire layers, however I don't know how many clients do this more eagerly with pulls.

---

> By the way stargz-snapshotter now has a zstdchunked mode. Does it mean that podman+stargz-snapshotter can lazy-pull a zstd:chunked image, or was this not integrated yet?

Podman can pull a `zstd:chunked` image by itself. eStargz to my knowledge requires image conversion with some extra metadata/content applied like with `gzip`. As noted some optimization features may not be supported for `zstd:chunked`.

You should still get the benefit of lazy pulling and prefetching that eStargz offers, which without the prefetching config step may be worse depending on if the image will try loading many files and incure high latency.

---

> does this mean I need zstdchunk, I heard about it I haven't been able to look into it.

From what I understand it will help cache content locally, at the expense of additional disk space? I'm not sure if there's a limit on cache size for eviction, or if the partial content is kept in a different store since it's not associated to any specific image.

> will stargz help with my unstable slow connection?

I am not sure if it has the same local cache, but assuming it does keep individual content it pulls then it should also help.

> Stock podman looses pretty much loses all progress when the connection drops, it will even lose blobs that already completed!

My understanding is that this is the same as with Docker, where the image you pull has the layers decompressed to storage, then discards the compressed layer from the registry rather than keeping it on disk.

When the image is pulled entirely with uncompressed layers stored, then in future pulls it can identify that an image layer already exists that matches by digest and skip pulling that again. But if the image doesn't successfully pull everything it doesn't keep a record and anything extracted may be at a different location? 🤷‍♂️ 

I believe this might differ with containerd image storage, which does keep the compressed image layers locally and those are uncompressed for the client such as Docker to run, using extra storage on disk.

> store plugin makes it sound like it's some kind of plugin that connects to some stargz repo, like it's a stargz app store. it's a configuration for storage under a section, a plugin, in the config file.

These originate from containerd, where they are plugins (extending functionality from core). Docker I think has referred to it as containerd image store integration. Docker's official docs doesn't have much on eStargz (_[except this](https://docs.docker.com/build/exporters/#compression) which is a bit outdated as it associates it only to `gzip`_).

---

> ok so looks like "partial pulls" have the benefit of not needing to pull the entire image when updating, instead pulling only the blobs that are updated
> 
> "Worse, every time the Fedora image—or any image—updates, you have to pull the entire image again, not just the differences."

`zstd:chunked` still requires pulling all layers for content it doesn't match AFAIK. I'm not sure if there's any optimization by clients to avoid pulling content that was overwritten/removed from subsequent layers, which is a benefit from eStargz should your image be built inefficiently.

As mentioned earlier, I think checksums metadata needs to match for it to be cached, so if something like mtime is included in that you'd still pull an otherwise equivalent file just because the mtime differed.

> > 
> 
> `zstd:chunked` still requires pulling all layers for content it doesn't match AFAIK. I'm not sure if there's any optimization by clients to avoid pulling content that was overwritten/removed from subsequent layers, which is a benefit from eStargz should your image be built inefficiently.
> 
> As mentioned earlier, I think checksums metadata needs to match for it to be cached, so if something like mtime is included in that you'd still pull an otherwise equivalent file just because the mtime differed.

but if you already have a file in the local storage, it won't be pulled again, so pulling the new layer requires less data.  Metadata changes (mtime, uid, gid...) do not cause the file to be fetched again

> Metadata changes (mtime, uid, gid...) do not cause the file to be fetched again

Good to know, that's a massive win then. It was a common annoyance with it's affect on image layers before I was aware of these enhancements :)
